last_result.effect: This asks the manager for information only; it does not change the EasyCrypt proof state.
last_result.result: The manager returned read-only context for the current goal.

content.preview:
=== `while` tactic — argument forms ===

Current proof mode: ambient

Form 1:  while (INVARIANT) (VARIANT).
  Use when: One-sided phoare/probabilistic loop: the proof is already inside a `phoare` obligation and the loop needs both an invariant and a decreasing integer measure.
  Example:  while (0 <= j <= N /\ oflist s = restr x j) (N - j).
  Note:     Do not append `: p`, `(p)`, or any probability argument. The second parenthesized argument is the termination measure; EC generates the probability/body side obligations after this step.

Form 2:  while (INVARIANT).
  Use when: Symmetric while: both sides have while loops that advance together. Invariant holds between iterations.
  Example:  while (={p, c, i, n} /\ OCC.gs{1} = RO.m{2}); auto.

Form 3:  while{1} (INVARIANT) (TERMINATION).
  Use when: One-sided while on LHS: LHS has a while, RHS doesn't (or has already been processed). Provide INVARIANT (holds each iteration) AND a TERMINATION measure (strictly decreasing integer that reaches 0 to end the loop).
  Example:  while{1} (0 <= i <= n) (n - i).
  Note:     Termination measure REQUIRED for one-sided while. EC won't accept without it.

Form 4:  while{2} (INVARIANT) (TERMINATION).
  Use when: One-sided while on RHS: symmetric to {1} form, just for RHS. Typical case: the RHS has a forgery-detection loop or lookup loop that the LHS doesn't.
  Example:  while{2} (0 <= i <= size ns /\ forged = exists j, 0 <= j < i /\ ...) (size ns - i).
  Note:     Common pattern: a forgery-detection or table-lookup loop on the RHS that scans for a witness while the LHS proceeds without it.

⚠️  Common mistake: Trying symmetric `sim` or `while (Inv)` when LHS and RHS programs are asymmetric (different number of loops, one-sided code). The fix is `while{1}` or `while{2}` with a termination measure. Detecting asymmetry: check if `proc; inline *; sim` leaves significant residual goal — if yes, programs diverged.

See also: seq, sim  (run `-tactic-forms <name>` for any of these)

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: setup statements appear before the current frontier; loop guards are part of the current proof shape; sampling steps are visible inside the frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.right_first: no right-side setup before this frontier
first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: Single-program (phoare/hoare) frontier; there is no second side to align.

rows.right: no right-side setup before this frontier
rows.proof_read: The left side has setup before the shared frontier; relate it to the precondition or carry the initialized state in the next invariant/cut.

rows.right: no matching right-side loop at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is the loop frontier; choose a while invariant that carries the state needed by the postcondition.

rows.right: no matching right-side sample at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a sampling frontier; choose a coupling or distribution argument that connects the sampled values.

rows.right: no matching right-side statement at this frontier
rows.proof_read: Only the left side has this frontier statement; account for it with the precondition, a one-sided transform, or the next invariant.

rows.right: no matching right-side statement at this frontier
rows.proof_read: Only the left side has this frontier statement; account for it with the precondition, a one-sided transform, or the next invariant.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

absorb_depth.sp_hint: sp 2 0 / wp -2 -0 absorbs the straight-line setup (2 left, 0 right) before the next frontier

branch_guards.rcond_forms: rcondt{1} 3 or rcondf{1} 3 (direction needs guard entailment — yours to decide)
rcondt{1} 3 or rcondf{1} 3 (direction needs guard entailment — yours to decide)

checks: Keep one-sided instrumentation as a map, not as a tactic recipe.

evidence: distribution or lossless token remains visible
obligation_families.effect: The remaining pure goal may include invertibility or lossless side conditions from an earlier sampling alignment.
obligation_families.limitations: No distribution lemma is selected by this surface.

translation_facts: Decorations identify the program memory from which a term is read.
Pretty-printer memory names and introduced proof-context names can differ.
ambient_memory_translation.effect: Local claims in the pure tail depend on the currently introduced memory binders as well as displayed term decorations.

pure_tail_surface.effect: Summarizes verifier-visible pure-goal structure after program frontier actions have been discharged or hidden by local proof steps.
limitations: This panel records syntactic and proof-state facts from the current view.
It does not choose a tactic or instantiate a lemma.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

notes.message: One-sided sampling residual: b{1} <$ {0,1}. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

steps.tactic: byphoare (_: i = i0 ==> res = a i0) => //.

proof_so_far.note: Your committed proof so far in THIS node, step-numbered — your own work. Use it to orient; do not re-derive steps already listed here.

evidence: pure_tail_surface is visible for the current goal
current proof state has no program-frontier work before the logical residual
limitations: does not select a destructor, witness, rewrite, or SMT lemma
reported checkpoints are recovery references, not a default route

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the lemmas that apply to an OPERATOR in your goal — project-local lemmas included (not just stdlib) — instead of guessing a lemma name.
ask_manager_for.returns: lemmas mentioning that operator, found by live EC `search` over the loaded context (statements; you pick which to apply)

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid form for call, while, seq, rnd, or rewrite.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid one-sided hoare/phoare loop form.
Mid-proof suffix may need indexed `wp` to absorb a tail before a cut.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp` or `wp`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken the pre/postcondition before closing.
A sampling step may need `rnd` to reduce it to a distribution/probability fact.

items.label: Before inline expansion #4
items.why_checkpoint: inline boundary; selecting it restores the proof state before this expansion
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #4 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

actions.label: operator_lemmas: <operator>
actions.description: Need the lemmas that apply to an OPERATOR in your goal — project-local lemmas included (not just stdlib) — instead of guessing a lemma name.
actions.eligibility_reason: operator query is derived from the current goal

actions.label: tactic_forms: <name>
actions.description: Need the valid form for call, while, seq, rnd, or rewrite.
actions.eligibility_reason: tactic family matches the current proof phase
